issues.description: The line 'dy/dx is not a fraction — it is a single symbol — but it behaves like one in many contexts' is the kind of nuance students remember as a contradiction. In Module 3 (chain rule) and later in u-substitution and separable ODEs, dy/dx will get split as dy = f'(x) dx. Without naming that ahead of time, students feel betrayed and decide the rule was a lie.
issues.fix: Soften to: 'dy/dx is a limit, not literally a fraction of two numbers — but it inherits the algebra of fractions because it came from one, which is why chain rule (dy/du · du/dx) and u-substitution work. We'll cash that in next module.' Plants the bridge instead of building a wall.

issues.description: Symmetric difference quotient is introduced as the way to 'estimate a derivative' without explicitly saying it is an approximation, not the definition. A reasonable student walks away thinking f'(2) ≈ (f(2.01)−f(1.99))/0.02 IS the derivative. On the AP exam they then mis-answer a 'which limit equals f'(a)' question because they pattern-match to the symmetric form, which is NOT the College Board's defining limit.
issues.fix: Add one sentence: 'This is an estimate from data — the true derivative is still the limit as h→0 from section 05. The symmetric form just averages the left and right secants for a tighter numerical guess.' Also keep the definition slide (05) visible side-by-side.

issues.description: Only |x|'s corner is shown as the counterexample to 'continuous ⇒ differentiable.' That plants the misconception that 'no corner = differentiable.' Students then miss AP questions involving (a) cusps like y = x^(2/3) at 0, (b) vertical tangents like y = x^(1/3) at 0, and (c) jump/removable discontinuities where the function isn't even continuous. The FUN 2.4 topic explicitly tests all three failure modes, not just corners.
issues.fix: Expand the counterexample panel to a 2x2 mini-grid: corner (|x|), cusp (x^(2/3)), vertical tangent (x^(1/3)), and 'not even continuous so automatically not differentiable.' Even tiny thumbnails are enough — students need to recognize all four shapes.

issues.description: Mnemonic given is 'low d-high minus high d-low, over low-squared,' which encodes (g·f' − f·g')/g^2. That equals (f'·g − f·g')/g^2 by commutativity, so it's correct — but the slide formula writes f'·g first while the mnemonic says 'low d-high' (i.e., g·f') first. Visually the order of factors does not match left-to-right between the spoken mnemonic and the written formula. Sharp students notice and second-guess.
issues.fix: Either rewrite formula as (g·f' − f·g')/g^2 to match the mnemonic word order, OR change mnemonic to 'd-high times low minus high times d-low, all over low-squared.' Pick one and align.

issues.description: 'Co-functions get negatives' is a useful memory hook but slightly misleading: sec x is NOT a co-function and its derivative sec x · tan x has no negative — fine. But csc x IS a co-function and its derivative −csc x · cot x is negative — fine. The rule actually holds for cos, cot, csc. The risk is a student over-generalizing 'anything with co- gets a minus' to inverse trig later (arccos has a minus, but arccot's sign convention varies by textbook — a real gotcha in BC/college).
issues.fix: Tighten phrasing to: 'Among the six trig derivatives, the three co-functions — cos, cot, csc — carry a minus. Sec does not.' Avoid the open-ended 'co-functions get negatives' which invites bad transfer.

issues.description: The pause-and-try is at section 15 of 18, after all six rules are taught and right before the recap. By that point a student has been passively absorbing rules for ~6 minutes with no chance to test themselves. The first active-recall moment is too late. Also, only the product rule is checked — quotient rule, the section flagged as the most error-prone, gets no pause.
issues.fix: Move 15/16 to right after section 11 (product rule), and add a second pause after section 13 testing quotient rule on something like d/dx[(x^2+1)/x]. Spaced practice beats one big pause at the end.

issues.description: 18 sections in ~9 minutes averages 30 seconds each — tight but doable. The padding risk is 17_recap immediately followed by 18_path, both of which feel like outros. Combined with the pause/reveal pair (15/16) also at the end, the last 4 sections are all low-information-density. A student's attention drops in the final third, exactly when the recap is trying to land.
issues.fix: Merge 17_recap and 18_path into one closing slide, or move the pause-pair earlier (per the section 15 fix) so the lesson lands on the recap as the last beat. Don't end on a path/CTA slide for a content lesson.

issues.description: The derivative table lists ln x with derivative 1/x but doesn't mention the domain restriction (x > 0). When students later see d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x on the AP exam or in integration (∫ 1/x dx = ln|x| + C), they get confused about why the absolute value appears. Tiny note now saves a misconception later.
issues.fix: Add a small footnote on the table: 'd/dx[ln x] = 1/x for x > 0. The natural log is only defined for positive inputs — we'll generalize with |x| when we integrate.'

overall_comment: Solid lesson with one genuinely concerning gap: the differentiability counterexample only shows a corner, which plants 'no corner = differentiable' and will cost students points on cusp/vertical-tangent AP questions. The other issues are smoothing — the dy/dx framing, the quotient-rule mnemonic ordering, and the late-and-lonely pause — that would tighten the lesson but won't damage a student if left as-is.
